front: What is a quantifier in logic?
back: A quantifier is a logical operator that specifies the quantity of specimens in the domain of discourse that satisfy an open formula. The two fundamental types are the universal quantifier and the existential quantifier.

front: What symbol represents the universal quantifier and what does it mean?
back: The universal quantifier is represented by the symbol ∀. It means 'for all' or 'for every'. The statement ∀x P(x) is true if P(x) is true for every element x in the domain.

front: What symbol represents the existential quantifier and what does it mean?
back: The existential quantifier is represented by the symbol ∃. It means 'there exists'. The statement ∃x P(x) is true if there exists at least one element x in the domain for which P(x) is true.

front: What is the negation of the universal quantifier statement ∀x P(x)?
back: The negation of ∀x P(x) is ∃x ¬P(x). It is not the case that P(x) is true for all x if and only if there exists at least one x for which P(x) is false.

front: What is the negation of the existential quantifier statement ∃x P(x)?
back: The negation of ∃x P(x) is ∀x ¬P(x). It is not the case that there exists an x for which P(x) is true if and only if P(x) is false for every x in the domain.
